meta.title: Life Skills & Independent Living | Who's to Say ? Foundation
meta.description: Mission-oriented independence preparation, routines, and everyday skill-building with appropriate service boundaries.

hero.title: Life Skills & Independent Living Preparation
hero.subtitle: Everyday skills for confidence and independence — organized as a mission or approved-provider lane, not a default public creative-service purchase.
hero.bestFor: People preparing for more independence at home and in the community, when the proper support lane and supervision plan are in place.

blocks.html: Life Skills & Independent Living Preparation focuses on everyday habits and routines that support independence. Because this work may involve direct support, supervision, or habilitative skill-building, it must be matched to the appropriate service lane before services begin.

items: Using calendars, alarms, or visual schedules
Breaking a routine into clear steps
Simple meal-prep concepts and kitchen safety
Practicing purchases only when consent and service boundaries are clear
Practicing safe planning before community activities
Arranging direct support or transportation separately when required

blocks.html: This pathway should not be presented as a default Goods & Services creative project. When the work is primarily life skills, community support, supervision, or habilitation, it should be routed to the appropriate approved service lane, provider partner, private-pay arrangement, grant, or mission-funded support.

sessionStyle.title: Session Style
items: Requires clear support and service boundaries

getStarted.title: Get Started
getStarted.prompt: Want to discuss the right lane for independence goals?

blocks.html: Families and teams should first identify whether the requested work is direct support, skill-building, supervision, a creative project, or another service. The correct lane should be chosen before scheduling begins.

Life-skills and independence supports may overlap with defined service categories. Use the proper approved provider, partner, or funding source when the support is not a public creative-service purchase.
